title: Ковариантность интерфейсов: out T / in T

cards.prompt: IEnumerable<out T> is covariant, so IEnumerable<string> assigns to IEnumerable<object> (unlike the invariant List<T>); the elements are unchanged, so Count() is 3 and First() is a
cards.code: using System; using System.Collections.Generic; using System.Linq; IEnumerable<string> strings = new List<string>{"a","bb","ccc"}; IEnumerable<object> objects = strings; Console.WriteLine($"{objects.Count()} {objects.First()}");

cards.prompt: A custom covariant interface IProducer<out T> (T only in return position) lets IProducer<Dog> assign to IProducer<Animal>; the factory still builds a real Dog, so Produce().Sound is woof and is Dog is True
cards.code: using System; interface IProducer<out T> { T Produce(); } class Animal { public virtual string Sound => "..."; } class Dog : Animal { public override string Sound => "woof"; } class DogFarm : IProducer<Dog> { public Dog Produce() => new Dog(); } IProducer<Dog> pd = new DogFarm(); IProducer<Animal> pa = pd; Console.WriteLine($"{pa.Produce().Sound} {pa.Produce() is Dog}");

cards.prompt: A custom contravariant interface IConsumer<in T> (T only in parameter position) lets IConsumer<Animal> assign to IConsumer<Dog>; the method takes an Animal and reads the base field, printing consumed: animal
cards.code: using System; interface IConsumer<in T> { void Consume(T item); } class Animal { public string Kind = "animal"; } class Dog : Animal { } class AnimalPrinter : IConsumer<Animal> { public void Consume(Animal a) => Console.WriteLine("consumed: " + a.Kind); } IConsumer<Animal> ca = new AnimalPrinter(); IConsumer<Dog> cd = ca; cd.Consume(new Dog());
